concepts.title: Oxides and Hydroxides
concepts.conceptBody:
Compounds Of Alkali Earth Metals

1. Oxides and Hydroxides Preparation:

1. $2\text{Be}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{BeO}$

$2\text{Ca}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{CaO}$

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\,$

$\text{MgC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{MgO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

2. $\text{MgO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Mg}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$

$\text{CaO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$

Note: Basic strength of hydroxides increase going down the group.

$\text{Be}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}<\text{Mg}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}<\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}<\text{Sr}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}<\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$

Properties: 

(1) $\text{BeO}$ is insoluble in ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ but dissolves in acid and alkali

$\text{BeO}+2\text{HCl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{BeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{BeO}+\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{Sodium Beryllate}}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Be}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{CaO}$ combines with solid acidic oxides at high temperature

$\text{CaO}+\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaS}{{\text{i}}_{3}}{{O}_{3}}$

$6\text{CaO}+{{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{10}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{C}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}$

(2) Ca(OH) 2 is called lime water or slaked lime and $\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}$ is called baryta water, Lime water or baryta turns milky by $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ milky }}{\mathop{\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{Ba}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ milky }}{\mathop{\text{BaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Milky ness disappears when excess of CO 2 is passed, due to formation of soluble bicarbonates

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\underset{\text{ excess }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ soluble }}{\mathop{\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}}}\,$

$2\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\underset{\begin{smallmatrix} \text{ Calcium } \\ \text{ hypochlorite } \end{smallmatrix}}{\mathop{\text{Ca}{{(\text{ClO})}_{2}}}}\,+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

concepts.title: Carbonates and Bicarbonates
concepts.conceptBody:
Bicarbonates and Carbonates 

  • The carbonates are all ionic, but due to excessive hydration of$\text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}},\text{BeC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ is unusual because it contains the hydrated ion ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2+}}$
  • $\text{BeC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ decomposes at low temperature and is placed in the atmosphere of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{BeC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{BeO}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$
Thermal stability increases with increase in atomic weight down the group or with increasing cationic size
$\text{BeC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}<\text{MgC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}<\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}<\text{SrC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}<\text{BaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$
$\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$ and $\text{Mg}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$ exist in water causing temporary hardness which can be removed as insoluble carbonates by boiling
$\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(\downarrow )+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$
$\text{Mg}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{MgC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(\downarrow )+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

  • $\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{ and Mg}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$are amphoteric thus neutralized by acid as well as base:

$\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+2~{{\text{W}}_{2}}$
$\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(\downarrow )+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

concepts.title: Sulphates
concepts.conceptBody:
Sulphates

  • The solubility of the sulphates in water decreases down the group.

$\underbrace{\text{BeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}>\text{MgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}_{\text{Soluble }}>\underbrace{\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}_{\begin{smallmatrix} \text{ Sparingly } \\ \text{ soluble } \end{smallmatrix}}>\underbrace{\text{SrS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}>\text{BeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}_{\text{in soluble }}$

High solubilities of $\text{BeS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ and $\text{MgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ are due to the high enthalpy of hydration of the smaller $\text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}$ and $\text{M}{{\text{g}}^{2+}}$ ions, which over comes the lattice energy factors.

  • The sulphates decompose into oxides and $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

$\text{MS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{MO}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

  • $\text{MgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$is called Epsom Salt Although correct formulation is

$\left[ \text{Mg}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{6}} \right]\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{MgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 7{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{150{}^\circ C}\text{MgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow[{}]{200{}^\circ C}$ $\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{MgO}\xrightarrow[heating]{strong}\text{MgS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

Note: The reason that it first changes to monohydrate lies in the above give formulation $\left[ \text{Mg}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{6}} \right]\quad \text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

  • $\text{CaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\cdot 2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is called gypsum.

$\underset{\text{gypsum}}{\mathop{CaS{{O}_{4}}\cdot 2{{H}_{2}}O}}\,\xrightarrow[{}]{150{}^\circ C}\underset{\text{plaster of paris}}{\mathop{CaS{{O}_{4}}\cdot \frac{1}{2}{{H}_{2}}O}}\,\xrightarrow[{}]{200{}^\circ C}\underset{\text{anhydrite}}{\mathop{CaS{{O}_{4}}}}\,\xrightarrow[{}]{1100{}^\circ C}CaO+S{{O}_{3}}$

$CaS{{O}_{4}}+4C\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}CaS+4CO$
